topic: The difference between a model that memorizes and one that generalizes, told without equations
title: Your Warehouse AI Isn't Smart. It Just Remembers Every Last Pallet.
summary: This breaks the unspoken operational difference between logistics AI that memorizes past shipments and one that adapts when everything breaks.
listicle: A memorizing model will nail average Tuesday load plans, then completely break the first time a port delays 12 container ships.
Generalization is not 'smarts'. It is the ability to guess correctly about things it has never seen before.
You test this by feeding it a scenario that literally never existed in your training data. No exceptions.
9 out of 10 logistics AI tools on the market right now are just fancy lookup tables with good marketing.
Nobody advertises this difference. It is the single most important question you can ask a vendor.
thread: Nobody got fired for buying the memorizing model. Until the storm hits.
Most vendors don't even know which type of model they actually built.
You don't need a data scientist to test this. Just make up a weird edge case.
Generalization costs 10x more to train. Nobody mentions that either.

criticNotes.note: Correctly avoids framing generalization as magic. This is the first plain explanation I've seen that doesn't lie about what it actually is.
Add one concrete vendor test script next post. Every sales team will lie about this when asked directly.
No jargon, no gotchas. The pallet line lands better than any textbook definition ever could.
